turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments in San Diego Gas & Electric Company v. City of San Diego. Mr. Goebel, you may proceed when you're ready. ORAL ARGUMENT OF LOUIS E. GOEBEL, ESQ., ON BEHALF OF THE APPELLANT

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Our case this morning deals with the availability of a money damage remedy for just compensation when the oppressive of all practical beneficial or economic use of his property. In particular, this case presents a question which was left open by the decision of this Court last term in Agins v. City of

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's possible, Your Honor. In this case we had a fully developed trial record as opposed to the Agins case. The trial court found a taking and that taking was affirmed by the California Court of Appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, am I wrong that the latest order of the intermediate appellate court leaves open the question whe- there there was a taking?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I don't believe --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Initially, there was a finding, you know, that there was a taking, both by the initial court and by the appellate division, was there not?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But then I thought, when -- what's this strange practice of a transfer by your Supreme Court to the appellate court? That's not a remand, or is it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: It's a -- it is, in effect, a remand of the same case, Your Honor. intermediate appellate court's opinion anyway.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, and then, doesn't the appellate court send it back leaving still open the question of taking?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: They sent it back for reconsideration in light of their Agins decision. Now, it's our position that there was no question at that point in time what the basis for the second California Court of Appeal decision was. The California Court of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: See, what I'm reaching at, is there a final judgment here? That's -- do we have jurisdiction at all?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On the taking question?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: In regard to this taking away this judgment, the California Court of Appeal specifically stated that a local agency's arbitrary, unconstitutional exercise of the police power does not require compensation. It was that premise which caused the California Court of Appeal to do a brought this appeal to this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Goebel, your client, as I understand it, wants to build a power plant on this land. Is that right?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Your Honor, that was the original in­ tention at the time of acquisition of this property in 1966. However --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The taking, the theory of the taking, is that you're denied the right to build the plant you planned on building?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Build the plant or some other indus­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, supposing, when the case goes back to the trial court, the trial court enters an order that says, let them build either a power plant or some other industrial use. Would you be complaining about that?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: As it happens -- I'll answer' that on1 twc

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: A well-planned, designed industrial park.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right. Supposing, when you go back, the trial judge enters an order and says that this would be an unconstitutional taking unless the property owner is permit­ ted to build precisely that, and you will argue for the right to do that? You do have the right to do that on remand, don't you?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's not the California practice as I understand it, Your Honor. The California practice for these system finds the action previously taken by the municipality to be improper, the case is simply remanded to the municipality for, to be dealt with entirely anew.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't think you get any relief at all on remand? park. Would he have power to grant you that relief?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I assume there is power andjurisdiction to grant that relief, Your Honor. It's not the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do we know he's not going to do it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I think that in light of the decision in the Agins case and in our case when it was previously before the courts, that that's not likely to happen.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Goebel, what's the significance of that sentence in the second opinion of the appellate court, perty, nor has it asked the:City what, development might be permitted." What's that all about?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It sounds like you didn't exhaust your administrative remedies.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Your Honor, it is correct that the

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I'm not certain, Your Honor. I think in the context of this remand and the statement that I read to Your Honor previously, that it is clear that the action of the Court of Appeal, what caused the Court of Appeal on the same record to take a 180 difference in view was the fact that the California Supreme Court had said, even an arbitrary, unconsti­ tutional exercise of the police power does not require compen­ sation .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me be a little more specific. When did your client, the utility, reach the decision not to build

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: It would have been in the early '70s, Your Honor, prior to the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you know what the basis of that deci­ sion was?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: A number of changing concerns b y 't h e

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have you had any offers to purchase?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But, in any event, the appellant never sought approval for a nuclear plant?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. And it is our position that in effect, as a matter of law, beyond dis­ pute, at the conclusion of the totality of the City's conduct in June of 1973, would have been totally impractical and fu­ tile to do so by reason of the consistency policy which the space designation on a particular property, they required any building permit and any subdivision map on that property to be consistent with the open space designation. We say, how can any industrial use be consistent with open space?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And yet they purported to bring within the possibility a nuclear power plant.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: We believe that was a cynical -- I take it, repeal this ordinance? It could now, couldn't it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: It could repeal it and modify it; yes, Your Honor. ordinance. Now, if that were to happen, would the holding as to compensation mean you could not have compensation for the period that you've been denied the use of the property to build a nuclear plant, whatever that period is, a year or two, something?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Since that was not the case before the Court, we can't say for sure, Your Honor. There is not any significant precedent in California for interim damages in this kind of a situation. The typical situation where there's a remand and an invalidation is no interim damages. you don't collect damages, you seek a writ of mandate.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Agins is the controlling precedent, and in our view that's the precedent that caused the Court of Appeal to do a 180 and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Agins is not before us, is it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Agins is not before us, is it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I want you to declare -- HR. GOEBEL: In Agins, this Court, as I understand the opinion, found that there was no taking, and therefore did not reach the question of whether a state could limit a pro­ perty owner whose property has been taken by regulatory con­ duct to equitable remedies only. We don't ask that you re­ verse Agins. We simply ask that you confirm that the Fifth Amendment means what it says, that when there's a taking of property, that just compensation is required, and just compen­ sation means money damages.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you want us to write an essay?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: No, Your Honor, we'd simply like to have the money judgment affirmed on the basis of the Fifth Amendment and settle precedent of this Court from Pennsylvania Coal to Penn Central.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose you agreed with me that -- or agreed with the suggestion that there is no final decision on taking in the California courts. Wouldn't Agins suggest that perhaps we shouldn't decide the inverse condemnation question, that we should just say, there is no taking? Or remand until — or just don't decide the case until there is a decision on HR. GOEBEL: I can't put my finger on the fact that there is a final decision of the California courts that there is a taking. My position is that there was a taking in California established in the trial court and affirmed on the Court of Appeal until the California Supreme Court changed things. It was that part of the judgment --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that finding was never disavowed or reversed, was it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I n e f f e c t it w a s , because the conse­ quence of that taking, that is, the flowing of money damages Appeal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, your California court has held that when a taking is the result of the unconstitutional and arbi­ trary use of the exercise of the police power, there is no condemnation, although I expect that it would hold that when the taking is the result of the wholly legitimate use of the taking power, there is. It's rather odd. the government takes private property for the use of building a post office or a city hall, or a public park. And it can be quite legitimate. You're still constitutionally entitled to compensation, even if the taking is wholly rightful.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But your California court has held, presumably, that while you are entitled to compensation when the taking is wholly rightful, you're not entitled to it when the taking is wrongful.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: We view that as a very bizarre --

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: -- position, Your Honor, and further­ more, I want to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that correctly characterize it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I think so. I want to make it clear in this case that we're not dealing solely with a state claim. dix .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: G1 of your jurisdictional statement.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Of the jurisdictional statement. It is also found in the Joint Appendix at page 63. But it's our position that this cause of action that was litigated, found by the trial court and affirmed by the Court of Appeal, was not only a state cause of action but a federal constitutional tort, whether we call it common law inverse condemnation, a Bivens tort, or in effect a 1983 civil rights cause of action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that you're mixing up two things. The Just Compensation Clause of the Constitu­ tion, of the Fifth Amendment, as incorporated in the Four­ teenth for the purposes of this case, presumes a perfectly lawful taking, not a tort at all. It Is simply an exercise of the power of government, of its eminent domain power, to take up private property for public purposes. Why do you have to

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's all right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's your claim.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's the essence of it, Your Honor, whatever label we put on i t , it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But assume that it was perfectly clear in the California courts that the courts had decided, (a) there is a taking; (b) that you're not entitled to any money, however; you're entitled only to an injunction. Now, let's just assume that. Wouldn't both issues be open here, whether there is a taking for federal constitutional purposes, and then only if there were, would we reach the other question?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor, because the sole question that's open in California courts as I under­ stand it is the question of whether declaratory or equitable relief might be available.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but we don't need to accept the Amendment purposes, Fifth and Fourteenth Amendment purposes?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ’ And so, isn't the other side entitled to court's position on taking, it's my position that we proved in the trial court -- and you can examine the record and affirm -- that there was a taking. It's a mixed question of fact and law. You can look at the trial record.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you lost below? MR. GOEBEL No, we won below. We proved --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but you lost -- you're an appellant here . MR. GOEBEL Yes .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No matter, how:: far below you go. MR. GOEBEL That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you were a loser. MR. GOEBEL Only in that court, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, where you were a loser. But the -- California courts to decide is whether or not they will choose on the basis of these facts to grant equitable relief. We say that even if they decide to grant equitable relief, that's not constitutionally adequate, because the Fifth Amendment re­ quires more.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You need more money?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: We want money, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what you want, money.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Goebel, your second and third cause of action in your complaint were petitions for mandate and the Court order the City of San Diego to set aside the rezoning and to set aside the adoption of the open space element of its general plan. As I understand it, on remand, the trial court may grant that relief, theoretically.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, if they grant that relief, how could there then have been a taking? Aren't the things that are set aside the things on which you rely to establish the taking?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but it's our position that the first cause of action established that a taking occurred and that we were entitled to litigate that -- of state law, w e ’re entitled to litigate the federal constitu­ tional tort aspect of those facts in state court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me put it more simply. If you get the relief you prayed for in paragraph 3 of your complaint, would you still contend there had been a taking?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of what? You have a total right to use your property any way you want to.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: But we have seven years after that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you might have a damage action for you have not alleged any intent to use the property in this period of time. All you describe here, is some long-range plan in the future that you say was denied you. Maybe you could amend your complaint to get damages, but I don't understand how there's a taking if you set aside that which constituted the taking?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor. I think we're entitled to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The interim damages too?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Interim damages. And I don't think that really puts us back in our position, because we're now, by that time we'd be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but are you entitled to the interim damages as the consequence of a taking, or just of deprivation of use for the seven or eight years?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: If we are to accept the California Supreme Court's dichotomy between these two concepts, I think we're left with the interim damages as a result of the interim deprivation of use.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not consequent upon a taking if the ordinance that caused all the trouble-, the difficulty for you. If that's invalidated, then there's no taking, is there? You'll be free to go ahead and erect your nuclear plant.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: When that finally becomes a fact, it'll brother Stewart suggested earlier, the government, whatever the unit may be, ends up with the property and you end up with money.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if the government doesn't end up with the property, because the ordinance is invalidated, then you keep the property. You can build your plant, and if you have any claim, why is it anything more than damages?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Your Honor, if you get a chance to go through the facts of this case, it's our position that we're nances. We're dealing with the synergistic effect of a course of conduct which resulted in a taking, much along the formula­ tion that was very well discussed in the Penn Central case, that these cases are to be decided on an ad hoc case-by-case determination. And when you take into consideration all the was originally sought by resolution of public convenience and necessity. I t ’s that synergistic effect of this course of conduct that we claim entitles us to compensation and it's that synergistic effect which we also assert renders the equitable remedies, or the so-called equitable remedies, inade­ quate . There has been a great deal of discussion of these so-called equitable remedies, and again, we believe that they are inadequate. about the last paragraph of the opinion of the supreme court of California, F14 in the blue brief, where it says, "Recog­ nizing that constitutionally protected property interest is here involved, we also accept the reasonable latitude which must be afforded public officials on planning for implementa­ tion of legitimate land use goals. These twin purposes will be served by preserving to the landowner in appropriate cases declaratory relief or mandamus remedies. However, the use of inverse condemnation with its imposition of money damages upon the public entity would in our view unwisely inhibit the proper and necessary exercise of a valid police power."

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: T h a t ’s my understanding, Your Honor, and that certainly was the understanding of the California Court of Appeal. They said it in so many words, at page 13 of the reply brief, or page 66 of the Joint Appendix: "A local entity's arbitrary, unconstitutional exercise of the police power which deprives the owner of the beneficial use of his land does not require compensation." They said it point-blank. That's the rule in California as we understand it. And it's that rule that violates the federal Constitution in our view, and that's why we're asking this Court to act. But, further on the question of the adequacy of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it -- to be complete, it should have said, but of course he can get his property back. Or, the restrictions may be invalidated.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's true. It goes on to say that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: ft offers the property owners --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it doesn't leave the property owner without any remedy whatsoever. He can get the restrictions invalidated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's -~i> so your answer

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: They did not say one way or the other.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that, let's assume that the California courts would say, you can't get -- make us buy the property at perty and pay you for it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: When you have, as in this case, a com­ bination of four or five factors which through their own synergy come together and have the impact that we claim was present in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I gather, Mr. Goebel, your client's no longer interested in building the plant; let the City keep the property; it wants the money.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That's correct. But again, as the trial court found and as we assert --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, can't the City back out if it wants to ?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: That question really hasn't been dealt with in California.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know, but can't it?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I would think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would be compelled to, I take it, if the courts were to invalidate this synergistic combination that you have.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: If you could do such a thing, Your a violation might have an effect upon the public treasur . It's this cost-benefit --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's not clear to me, and maybe I'm re­ peating myself, but it's not clear to me whether you argue that your client's entitled to money damages for a wrongful act of San Diego, or whether you're simply entitled to compen­ sation because San Diego has taken your real property. Now,

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Let me try to address that without get­ the Fifth Amendment requires compensation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Whether it's by reason of constitutional or unconstitutional acts. you would have simply said, please pay me for the property you've taken. MR. GOEBEL T h a t 's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not for any wrongful action on the part of San Diego, except insofar as it was wrong to not pay you. MR. GOEBEL W&ong to not pay. That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Goebel, it's of course perfectly clear' that if a sovereign takes and keeps property, he's got to pay for it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If he takes it rightfully. But is there any precedent at all that you've cited where the government has impaired the use of private property in attempting to serve some governmental purpose, and its action has later been held unconstitutional, and it's been required to pay money to the property owner?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Well, I think that's implicit in the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the Fifth Amendment deals with rightful taking, for the moment. If this -- have you got any case at all for your position, involving a wrongful inter­ ference with the use of property, where the government was re­ quired to pay the property owner even a dime?

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Well, I think that's implicit in the When it was decided that it hadn't, then the property owner was always entitled to damages for the tying up of his proper­ ty during the period that the case was between the government decided to take it and the time the case was decided.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: Well, the U.S. v. Causby is a similar case, except, of course, the appellants would say that involved aspects of physical invasion. But that's the type of case. There are a number of cases. Each case is different, though. That's why we've led to the standard that it's an ad hoc case

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, in Causby it was held that the government was within its rights in flying the airplanes over the property, and that what it did was rightful but it caused and they didn't pay for it. In effect, our position is, this property has been devoted to public use since 1973, has served its purpose, the same purpose they wanted to condemn it for in the first place. It's open space.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You have an action for rent.

turns.name: GOEBEL
turns.text: I'd like to reserve the rest of my time.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Your time is entirely used up, counsel. Mr. Sumption. ORAL ARGUMENT OF C. ALAN SUMPTION, ESQ., ON BEHALF OF THE APPELLEES the Court: Briefly, I'd like to go over some of what I consider to be the important relevant facts in this case, because -- theories of liability and seeking of relief against the City of San Diego. We're talking about land use regulation by way of zoning on approximately half of the 214 acres of the proper­ ty that the trial court said we had taken. We're talking about land use activity by way of planning, as opposed to zoning, by the adoption of an open space plan, mandated by the State of California, required by all local governments, to attempt to plan for the preservation, whether it be through regulation or acquisition, of open space lands that are currently being diminished in the State of California.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the plan forbid the owner to make anything of an open space without the approval of the municipal plan?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: That's one of the issues that's in contention, Your Honor. It's our position it does not. The definitions cited in the open space plan, there's really no one single definition, but the plan recites that for most pur­ poses the definition is something to the effect that the pro­ perty shown as open space should remain in its essential, of the major issues, I think, in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that's a factual issue, and this Court isn't here to decide factual issues.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, except in the circum­ stance of this case and the way it's come up, the City of San Diego appealed the trial court's findings of fact in the Court of Appeal and on up on the basis that there was no substantial evidence to support them, and the question, the findings on a taking, and the question of whether or not the Gas S Electric Company was excused from applying for some relief to avoid the intertwined and mixed with legal questions that I don't think you can completely separate them out from factual findings .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it's certainly not a factual questior to say that if the City says that the 214 acres of the San Diego power company land that's presently open is hereafter a park with access to everybody, and no compensation is to be paid.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: The plan does not say that, and no­ used for X -- excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the difference if they declared it a park and put a lot of park benches in it and let the public use it?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: The difference would be that the public is then actually using the property. And that's one of the bases for the distinction between the power of eminent domain and the police power used to regulate land use by pro­ hibiting certain natural excuses. lic is not using it by --

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: The public is not using it in any physical sense. The property was vacant before the City of San Diego designated it for potential acquisition in its open space plan. It was unused and the public was enjoying it by driving by the freeway ana looking out to the ocean then just right to go on that property. We did not require there be any paths, pedestrian hikers' trails, bicycle paths, anything of that sort. It was merely to preserve and protect land that otherwise would have been developed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would the City have done had the utility attempted to develop the 214 acres in a way you would normally expect a utility to normally develop such acreage?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Your Honor, that's something that from this record we don't know and we will never know until the Gas £ Electric Company comes to the City and gives it the plan, to weigh and balance the important public interest ver­ sus the private property interest of this landowner to deter­ mine what is a reasonable use of the property, whether or not and claiming that we had somehow deceived them because we knew they had bought the property for construction of a power plant and that we somehow pulled the rug out from under them. The first time we ever heard that the Gas & Electric Company was thinking of other industrial uses for this property was at trial when they paraded out an industrial park development plan. They had never gone to the city planners, the staff, or the City Council and said, what do you think about the possi­ bility of us doing this? We can no longer use the property for a power plant site. tional use permit, they could have built -- they could make any use that a public utility can do by way of structures , buildings, or uses, by conditional use permit. Even on the agricultural zoned portions of the property, which were zoned agricultural when they bought the property, half of it was zoned industrial when they bought it, half of it was zoned well, first of all, from a procedural standpoint in California of a hearing in the California Supreme Court. When the California Supreme Court granted that hearing under California force and effect the Court of Appeal opinion which had affirmec the trial court's findings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But not the Superior Court opinion?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, that is gone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the Supreme Court Of'California in ordinary proceedings vacated the intermediate appellate court opinion, but left standing, reversed, or affirmed the Superior Court opinion?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I don't believe that's so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, just a granting of a hearing doesn't vacate the findings of the Superior Court. Is that what aside the findings?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I'm sorry. I didn't follow that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose the intermediate court had set aside the findings of the trial court?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes; which we had asked them to do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And suppose it had -- and then there's a grant of a hearing by the California Supreme Court, which vacates the judgment of the intermediate appellate court,

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Perhaps there's a semantical differ­ ence, but it doesn't -- my understanding is it does not rein­ state them, the court is still open to determine from the record whether there is evidence to support those findings of fact. And we contended that there was no --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they aren't vacated until and unless they reinstate them, are they?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Well, that could be, Your Honor. But in California?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Your Honor, I think transfer and remand, as far as I can tell, are one and the same.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Same thing?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I don't know why they use the differ­ ent terminology, because the California Supreme Court, I think, does use "remand" as well. But when the case went back, it's not a -- obviously, from reading it, it's not a model of clarity, but I can see no other interpretation from the lan­ guage, especially when we were contending that the Gas £ zoning had never been applied to them, and in light of the California Supreme Court's decision in the Agins case,then it goes back to the Court of Appeal and they expressly state that, Company did in its complaint seek these remedies, asserting that the City had arbitrarily exercised its police power by enacting an unconstitutional zoning law and general plan ele­ ments. And then it says, "or by applying the zoning." How­ ever, on the present record, these are disputed fact issues not covered, so I read that as the Court of Appeal saying there is no sufficient evidence on this record from which the gas company's argument that we had applied these regulations arbi­ trarily is supported.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what then do you understand the Court

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: They go on to say that if the Gas £ Electric Company wants to pursue its equitable remedies, to set aside these land use and planning decisions , that they -- the issues as to whether or not there was a taking.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I would think you would have dis­ missed the case until they had.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. You're right. In other words, they should not be able to pursue their judicial remedies until they have exhausted their administrative reme­ dies, which means finding out from the City of San Diego what they might or might not be able to do with this property. Then, if the conclusion results that there's no reasonable use that the city's going to allow them, because we want to buy this property for open space, they can -- you know, they've exhausted their relief, and the Court will not have to sit as a planning board or body, the trial court, as it did in the first trial of this case, and it can determine whether or not there was a taking and if there was a taking, what follows? What's the remedy?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, aren't you relying on an inference when there were originally two findings, or rather finding.by the trial court and acceptance of that finding by the Cali­ fornia intermediate appellate court, that it would have been futile? The California Supreme Court didn't say anything to the contrary.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Well, the California Supreme Court never really answered any question. They simply sent the case back after they decided Agins by saying, take another look at

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying that all findings have beer vacated in this case?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: As far as the taking question which in -- a subsidiary question of whether there was a taking and the failure to exhaust administrative remedies, and the futility --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could you identify by number the findings that have been vacated?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: It would take me a few minutes to have applied for any development. Those are the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The only basis for your argument, I judge from what you have said, is the final paragraph in the four- page opinion of the Court of Appeal on its second considera­ tion of this case?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, I can see no reason why the court would have said that they have not applied for any relief or found out what they could do with the property, unless they meant -- read it that rejects Finding No. 29, which in substance says that the owner already has been deprived of all practical use of his property. What language in that paragraph states -- ?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: No, there's nothing specific that says that. They don't refer to any findings either generally -- either by the word "findings" or by the number, specifi­ cally . written.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: That's right. And so, you eliminate that and then you take what the Court of Appeal is saying on its second opinion that's being appealed from, and they are mentioning the fact that they have not applied for any relief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What I don't understand is that the California Supreme Court decision in Agins didn't deal at all with these factual findings. Did -- and I say this from principles of law, that it would appear caused it to remand the California Supreme Court of the findings, and no review, again, of findings by your intermediate appellate court. If there's any authority in your brief that says "A local entity's arbitrary unconstitutional exer­ cise of the police power which deprives the owner of the beneficial use of his land does not require compen­ sation . . . " That's the way they understand the law of California and isn't that the issue before us? Whether or not that's correct, as a matter of federal constitutional law?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I don't think the Court can get to that issue without finding that there was a taking, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the Court of Appeal certainly got

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They also got to the taking question. You took the same position in the lower courts and you lost. They didn't -- they said, whatever the Company should or shouldn't have done, we nevertheless -- there is no barrier to our reaching the taking question. They reached it.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I realize that. state courts.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: But now, with the Court of Appeal's second opinion, they seem to be retracting from that posi­ tion --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it doesn't sound like it from what Justice Stewart read. It sounds like they stuck right to their guns.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but, don't you have to read the opinion in the light of the allegations of the complaint? that Count One of the complaint does not state a cause of action. That's the only count on which they ask for damages and that was the taking theory, compensation for the taking. There was no allegation in the complaint asking for interim damages, so there was no occasion for the courts of appeals to rule on whether not damages for loss of interim use would would follow with general damages under the taking clause, would it not?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: No, I don't believe that interim damages would necessarily be awarded under the taking clause if --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, you think the City could an­ nounce that as of January 1, 1973, this particular 215 acres is a park to which all of the citizens of California shall have, access, and then on January 1, 1978, five years later, pay the company the fair value of the park? California, plus seven percent interest per annum from the date of the taking, plus attorneys' fees and expert witness fees, if they have to go to court to prove that there was a taking. But I would caution Your Honor that that's not the facts in this case, as far as us declaring that that was a park. of the Court of Appeal's opinion?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: On what issue or aspect? remedies would be futile? You have to rely on that one sen­ tence in which they make reference to the fact that actually the property owner never did apply for any administrative relief.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But surely, earlier they said, didn't have to because it'd be futile to do it.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Well, they said earlier in a decision that it's w i p e d off. the books and no longer exists.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you have to -- you really have to

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: As far as interpreting what the Court of Appeal, was the basis of its ruling, that's all we have, and it's not entirely clear.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, you think that the complaint in this case challenged the constitutionality of a local ordinance?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Of a local ordinance? It did. that, but that's just that there isn't any final judgment on the taking issue.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But there was a straightforward challenge to a local ordinance on the constitutional grounds?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, there was.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you suggesting that they can do --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the complaint even mention the Federal Constitution?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I don't recall specifically if the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't think it does.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: -- mentions the Federal Constitution. It may just say constitutional deprivation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn't even say. that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you suggesting the power company can get in the beginning? Have any of these interim decisions foreclosed them of any right they had in 1973? MR, SUMPTION: On the question of damages, let me answer it this way, Your Honor. And I think that's the only particular question where that statement would be . No, there is probably some difference as a result of the Court of Appeal and the California Supreme Court's decision in Agins.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask another question before you take up another point? If we should conclude that Findings been a taking?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: No, I would not agree that there has been a taking.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you develop that a little bit? One finding- states that no practical use can be made of the property. The other states that it would be futile to make an^ a denial of property without due process of law. But if you're: meaning a taking from the eminent domain sense, that's where our position is that there is a distinction. And I think there seems to me to be a lot of confusion by decisions on the ten­ dency to blur the two distinct powers, the police power and the power of eminent domain. They are not, it's not a differ­ ence in degree. -- Excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just so I can understand you, you think that if a state or one of its subdivisions characterizes its action as an exercise of a police power, then the compensation

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Your Honor, it's not how we charac­ terize, it's not how we characterize it. It's whether or not we are regulating through land use means and telling the land-, owner what uses may not occur or may be prohibited as -- and that's the police power, as opposed to the power of eminent domain, which is, we are taking your property for public use. said, this crosses the line, this is a taking, in spite of the fact that the State has called it a regulation.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but the Court did not award money damages and title to the property. They said that the police power regulation could not stand up or it would con­ stitute a taking. And the Constitution is satisfied if the re­ striction is not enforced or wiped off the books. And so --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then, is it your view, Mr. Sumption, that this sentence which seems to me the dispositive holding of the Court of Appeal, that "A local entity's arbitrary un­ owner of the beneficial use of his land does not require com­ pensation," that that language contains a negative pregnant, so to speak, a negative implication that a local entity's rational and constitutional exercise of the police power, whicl deprives the owner of the beneficial use of his land, does re­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in an eminent domain sense, government could take property and then leave it undeveloped for years.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Well, we're speaking of taking in two complete different senses.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Sumption, let me put Mr. Justice Stewart's question somewhat differently. Suppose you owned, say, 50 acres of quite scenic property and the City had simply adopted an ordinance saying that, we would zone that property never put anything on it, we're just going to zone it for that purpose. But the public may wander through it, may fish in the ponds, may make such use as it wishes. Are you saying there would be no taking?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: No. I think that's what I'm trying to say is the distinguishing factor. If we somehow give the public

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: But he can. We're not physically taking the property away from him. If you mean, by no use, that he can't go on his own property, that's one thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Sumption, I wonder why you weren't satisfied with just defending the judgment of the Court of Appeal? Don't you consider you've won the case?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I am satisfied. affirmed on the grounds that the California courts have held that the landowner was wholly deprived of the use of his property and we accept those findings, but that in a sense that we agree with the Agins decision of the Supreme Court of California?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: I would be satisfied if this Court affirmed the ruling of the Court of Appeal below.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the California Court of Appeal has for the utility if it desires to retry the case and produce which is relied on and cited in this Court of Appeal opinion, that the remedy is equitable relief, not money damages, where a taking through governmental regulation has occurred. The case is not absolute. Counsel for appellant portrays it in his brief as though the California Supreme Court has created some sort of immunity. The California Supreme Court has still left the door open for money damages in land use cases in cer­ tain circumstances. One would be unreasonable precondemnation conduct. That is, a public entity wants to buy the property and they start, you know, doing things with an illicit motive in order to reduce the value so when we do get around to buy­ ing it we pay much less than what the property would otherwise be worth. The other situation, the California Supreme Court has said, where there is a denial of access to the property, they have still left open the door to money damages on that theory and where there is special and direct interference with the landowner's use of the property. And there is no evidence or facts on the record, even the findings of fact, that the City has unconstitutionally interfered with the use of this property.

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, has the California Supreme Court or the Court of Appeal precluded damages in that situation?

turns.name: SUMPTION
turns.text: Under those facts, without any actual use, without the other factors, denial of access or any direct and special interference with the landowner's attempt to use the property, I think that's a correct assessment, that the California Supreme Court would say, no, your remedy is to set aside the regulations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they get set aside but meanwhile the landowner has not been able to use it for the purpose he wanted. The zoning ordinance has effectively precluded his use
